retrieved_docs.title: Breastfeeding -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Milk contains microbiota like Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus, which confer anti-bacterial activity to the gut along with the delivered oligosaccharides. [16] [17] Furthermore, this facilitates the synthesis of essential nutrients such as vitamins B12, B6, K, and folate. Early introduction of breastfeeding will colonize the neonatal gut with these safe micro-organisms derived from the breastfeeding parent, crowding out organisms from the hospital room and other caretakers. Early rooming-in, where the neonate stays with the breastfeeding parent, is advised when possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Function -- Colostrum
retrieved_docs.content: It is the special yellowish milk only secreted in the first 2 to 3 days after the baby's birth, in small amounts (about 30–60 ml/day). It is abundant in white blood cells and antibodies, especially IgA, to fight off pathogens. It contains about 20 specific antibodies against E coli , salmonella, RSV, candida, streptococcus, staphylococcus, cryptosporidium, H pylori , etc. It has more protein, minerals, and fat-soluble vitamins (A, E, and K) than ordinary breast milk. Vitamin A is required to protect the eyes and form epithelium. It contains multiple growth factors. Insulin-like growth factors 1 and 2 are the most commonly found. The epidermal growth colostrum factor also forms the intestinal lining needed to absorb the nutrients. The neonate needs to be fed on colostrum and nothing else now. The composition of colostrum can be altered by maternal conditions such as eclampsia, diabetes, and anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Breastfeeding -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Lactoferrin is also transmitted via breastmilk; it confers immunoprotection against bacterial, viral, fungal, and parasitic infections by prohibiting the sequestration and degradation of iron by microbes. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Cellular Level -- Bioactive Substances
retrieved_docs.content: Breast milk contains white blood cells, immunoglobulin (Ig)A, IgG, IgM, cytokines, chemokines, growth factors, hormones, and antimicrobial substances. [4] The epidermal growth factor is present abundantly, and it stimulates the maturation of the lining of the infant's intestine. The IgA antibody destroys bacteria and protects the mucosal surface of the gut.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding correlates with decreased risk for cardiovascular, atopic, and infectious diseases. Additionally, breastfeeding is associated with a reduction in the risk of behavioral and developmental disorders. [2] Breast milk is vital for the newborn's development. However, breast milk pumping and storage have become widespread due to the increased number of working mothers. The maintenance of this practice is crucial to prevent the spread of pathogens through expressed milk. Preserving the integrity of the breast milk is essential, hence using proper hygiene is advised. Furthermore, the refrigerator can achieve short-term milk storage to prevent contamination. [15] The breast milk is contraindicated in the following conditions:

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Colostrum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Colostrum has more protein and fewer carbohydrates and fat than mature breast milk. Colostrum is rich in secretory immunoglobulin A (IgA), which helps to protect the infant from infection. Colostrum also helps to establish a normal gut microbiome in the infant. The bowel is considered sterile at birth.

retrieved_docs.title: Infant Nutrition Requirements and Options -- Issues of Concern -- Donor Milk
retrieved_docs.content: The roots of donor breastmilk lie in the concept of wet-nurses used as far back as the ancient world until the twentieth century. [33] In some developing world countries, it is still practiced in situations when access to the mother's breastmilk is not available. In the developed world, however, donor milk is now available from donor milk banks across the USA and in many parts of the developed world. The financial impact of using donor milk is quite significant, accounting for the slow adoption in the poorer countries. The quality of the donated breastmilk varies widely from person to person. [34] Hence most centers typically use pooled donor breastmilk from multiple donors in different stages of breastfeeding, intended to limit variation in the quality of breastmilk. [35] This donor breastmilk is usually processed by centralized milk banks after screening the donors and milk samples for communicable diseases (HIV, Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, etc.). In addition, the milk is usually sterilized by pasteurization. Most donor milk banks also require negative drug screens from the women, and the milk is screened before pooling. The processed milk is frozen using a cold-chain until it is ready to be used at the bedside. Individual institutions often have their written policy on the use of donor breastmilk.

retrieved_docs.title: Enterohormonal and Microbiota Pathophysiology of Obesity -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Newborns develop microbial colonization during the first month of life. [4] Infants delivered by cesarean section have lower gut bacterial counts than a natural birth, suggesting that the birth canal contributes to healthy microbiota. [26] Breast milk contains oligosaccharides and bacteria, including Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus , which may also be critical to developing the immune system. [27] [28] A progression of bacterial microflora occurs as the diet becomes more diverse and includes solid food, which is likely very important for immune system development and the prevention of certain autoimmune conditions. [28] [29] The geographic region where a child resides also impacts the microbiota during development. For example, microbial diversity is greater in African children than European Union children. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Colostrum -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Colostrum, the initial milk produced by the breast during pregnancy, can be expressed during the third trimester and begins to be secreted in larger volumes after birth. Lactogenesis II begins at 2 to 3 days postpartum and is often called transitional milk. This milk gradually changes from colostrum to mature milk. See Image. Colostrum to Breastmilk. The quantity of milk increases rapidly. The immunoglobulins and proteins decrease, and the lactose, fat, and calories increase. Lactogenesis III is mature milk and replaces the transitional milk. This milk is more bluish-white and not as thick. Mature milk contains 20 kcal/ounce and nutrients specific to meet the needs of the growing infant. It continues to have immunoglobulins and other antibacterial components. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In many societies, formula feeding is becoming more prominent, and breast milk has become the least preferred choice. Further evidence demonstrates how formula and bottle feeding lead to an increased risk of oral disease and alterations in oxygenation, thermoregulation, and the newborn's gut bacteria. [2] It is important to note that breast milk is not sufficient, and nutritional supplementation is sometimes needed for very low birth weight (VLBW) and preterm infants, as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Biological Weapon Toxicity -- Clinical Significance -- Brucellosis
retrieved_docs.content: Brucella species, ubiquitous gram-negative coccobacilli, cause brucellosis infections. Brucella is usually transmitted to humans from infected animals by contact or by consuming undercooked meat or unpasteurized dairy products. Person-to-person spread is rare; transmission through sexual contact and breastfeeding has been reported. Brucella are easily aerosolized, and few organisms are required to cause disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: In the initial week after pregnancy, the mother secretes colostrum, a thick yellow liquid. From days 7 to 14, the mother secretes transitional milk, a combination of colostrum and mature milk. After 2 weeks, the mature milk is formed and secreted. [4] Breast milk can be divided into the bluish-grey foremilk , present at the beginning of a feed, containing less fat, and the creamy white hindmilk secreted towards the end of a feed rich in fat. Breast milk can differ depending on maternal health and diet, environmental exposure, gestational age, and the infant's age. [5] Breast milk comprises 87% water, 3 to 5% fats, 6.9 to 7.2% lactose, 0.8 to 0.9% proteins, vitamins, minerals, and bioactive substances. [6] [4] [5] It can yield up to 60 to 75 kcal per 100 ml. The colostrum (breast milk in the early days) has more proteins and immunoglobulins. Mature milk contains a higher percentage of carbohydrates. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaerobic Infections -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anaerobes are part of the indigenous or native flora, particularly the oral cavity, human bowel, and female genital tract. The colonization with anaerobes varies with age, organ site, and environmental factors. For example, infants exclusively breastfed have gut flora with predominantly Bifidobacterium , with few Bacteroides and Enterococcus species. On the other hand, infants who are fed cow's milk have gut flora similar to that of an adult's, which contain gram-negative anaerobes and facultative bacilli. [3] [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Streptococcus Group B -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Research has also described horizontal transmission from mothers or other nosocomial and community contacts. [7] Research studies investigating the causes of late-onset GBS disease propose an acquired fecal-oral route of transmission. Whether late-onset disease results from an exogenous source, such as breast milk, or established colonization, or both, remains unclear. In addition to newborn infants, GBS can also cause invasive infections in those with a weakened immune system, such as pregnant women or immunocompromised adults with malignancy, diabetes mellitus, or HIV. Also, patients with either asplenia or functional asplenia, such as those with sickle cell disease, may be at risk for invasive GBS infection, given the spleen’s important role in neutralizing encapsulated organisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Mastitis -- Etiology -- Lactational Mastitis
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying causes of lactational mastitis are multifactorial. One potential contributor is the disruption of the breast microbiome, known as mammary dysbiosis, which may involve an imbalance of bacteria rather than a straightforward infection. [1] [2] Pathogenic bacteria (eg, Staphylococcus and Streptococcus ) are more frequently isolated from women with mastitis, but these bacteria are also present in healthy milk microbiomes, making their role complex. [2] Nipple trauma can facilitate bacterial entry, but studies show that many women with mastitis do not have visible nipple damage. [10] This evidence suggests that mastitis is not solely caused by retrograde bacterial spread through nipple cracks. Instead, a disrupted milk microbiome, influenced by factors like antibiotics, probiotics, hyperlactation, ductal narrowing, and the mechanical stress of breastfeeding, contributes to this inflammatory condition. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Breast Milk -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The concentration of protein in breast milk is lower than in animal milk. Higher protein content overloads the immature kidneys with waste nitrogen products. Breast milk contains higher whey protein levels than formula, making digestion easier for newborns. Infant formulas are high in casein, whereas cow's milk contains only 18% whey out of the total protein content. Furthermore, various whey proteins provide different functions necessary for the newborn. For example, immunoglobulin A antibody coats the gut surface and destroys bacteria. The lactoferrin and lysozymes help the immune system against pathogenic bacteria. [5] PRP stimulates the thymus to regulate the immune system in the body and stabilizes the hyperactive immune system due to autoimmune diseases and allergies in the body. Alpha-lactalbumin is vital for the synthesis of lactose and the binding of calcium and zinc ions. Bile-salt stimulated lipase in breast milk assists in the complete digestion of fat in the small intestine [10] as opposed to the fat in artificial milk, which is only partly digested. Glutamine is required to provide substrate (ketoglutaric acid) for the citric acid cycle, its use as a neurotransmitter in the brain, and its role as a primary energy substrate for intestinal cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2323
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified) is a neonatal condition that, together with a range of other neonatal disorders, defines the health challenges encountered during the neonatal period. Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, postpartum hemorrhage (نزف سري بعد الولادة), neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), and jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان) all occur within the neonatal timeframe. Recognizing that these entities—bacterial sepsis of the newborn, delayed‑conjugation jaundice, postpartum hemorrhage, respiratory tract hemorrhage, breast‑milk‑induced jaundice, and prematurity‑related jaundice—are interrelated manifestations of neonatal physiology and pathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, timely intervention, and optimal neonatal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1206
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified), Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation (including the specific form caused by delayed development of the conjugating system), نزيف حديثي الولادة (neonatal hemorrhage), and يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice) are all conditions that occur during the neonatal period. The qualifier “Neonatal” therefore applies to each of these entities, indicating that their onset, presentation, or occurrence is confined to the first weeks of life. Recognizing that these diverse disorders share the common temporal context of the neonatal stage helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and management strategies appropriate for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2324
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is classified as a neonatal condition. The qualifier “Neonatal (qualifier value)” is used to denote a range of disorders that occur in the newborn period, including several distinct types of neonatal jaundice: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory distress), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice resulting from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑associated jaundice). These interrelated neonatal entities share the common context of early‑life physiology and pathology, underscoring the importance of recognizing their overlapping presentations for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: neonatal period, anchors a spectrum of early‑life disorders such as hereditary metabolic disease (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome), multiple jaundice subtypes (neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic neonatal jaundice, hemolytic jaundice, perinatal jaundice, Rh‑alloimmunization‑related jaundice, prematurity‑associated jaundice), and hemorrhagic presentations (general neonatal hemorrhage, intracranial neonatal hemorrhage, umbilical bleeding after birth, respiratory‑tract bleeding). These hemorrhagic events are linked to an unspecified perinatal condition that may arise from perinatal infections, which include unspecified bacterial sepsis of the newborn and neonatal botulism. Laboratory findings described as “Below reference range” are interpreted in the context of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice, providing quantitative guidance for diagnosis. The disorder “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” is both a child of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2423
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is identified as a neonatal occurrence, indicating that this infectious condition manifests during the newborn period. Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is likewise described with a neonatal qualifier, signifying that the syndrome occurs in neonates. The neonatal period is also the context for several other specific conditions: neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ), breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), and jaundice associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these relationships emphasize that bacterial sepsis, Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, respiratory hemorrhage, and various forms of neonatal jaundice are all conditions that arise in the neonatal stage, underscoring the importance of recognizing and managing these disorders promptly in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: by aligning surgical, nutritional, epidemiologic, pharmacologic, pathophysiologic and preventive interventions across diverse obstetric, neonatal, cystic, cardiovascular, metabolic and infectious conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2140
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriële sepsis van pasgeborene, niet gespecificeerd is a neonatal condition that can occur in newborns. The neonatal (qualifier value) is associated with several neonatal disorders, including نزف سري بعد الولادة (post‑umbilical bleeding after birth), يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), يرقان نووي (physiologic jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (physiologic jaundice caused by immature bilirubin conjugation in fetuses or newborns), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (prematurity‑related jaundice). These interrelated conditions illustrate the spectrum of neonatal complications that may present in the early post‑natal period, ranging from infectious sepsis to various forms of jaundice, and underscore the importance of recognizing neonatal qualifiers in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial sepsis of the newborn and neonatal botulism are classified within “other perinatal conditions,” a group that also extends into infancy, indicating that some perinatal disorders persist beyond the immediate newborn phase. The neonatal period, a sub‑category of the fetal and/or neonatal period, provides the temporal framework for a wide spectrum of disorders that include hereditary metabolic diseases such as Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome and Ingedikte‑galsyndroom, as well as numerous forms of neonatal jaundice. Jaundice subtypes recorded in this window are: delayed conjugation of bilirubin, delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, premature‑birth‑associated jaundice, jaundice due to fetal‑type hemolysis, hemolytic jaundice, and perinatal jaundice. These jaundice entities are closely linked to the Arabic term يرقان وليدي (neonatal jaundice) and are often associated with Rh alloimmunization (تمنيع راهائي إِسوِي), a condition

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal period, defined as the fetal and/or newborn stage of life, is associated with a spectrum of infectious, hemorrhagic, and hemolytic disorders. Bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified) represents a serious infection occurring during this time. Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is a parent classification that encompasses both bleeding and hemolysis‑related conditions in the infant. Two recognized sub‑entities of this classification are Ingedikte‑galsyndroom (a syndrome) and Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (a disorder), each sharing the core pathophysiology of neonatal bleeding and hemolysis while exhibiting distinct clinical features. Hemorrhagic manifestations include loosened umbilical cord clamp with bleeding (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑delivery umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), newborn hemorrhage (نزيف حديثي الولادة), and neonatal respiratory tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Hemolytic and jaundice‑related presentations comprise hemolytic jaundice, perinatal jaundice, physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, jaundice from delayed development of the hepatic conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), pre‑term‑associated neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان), and jaundice secondary to erythroblastosis fetalis (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). These jaundice entities are often interpreted in the context of laboratory values that fall below reference ranges, with total hemoglobin concentration measurement providing a key laboratory basis for assessing the severity of hemolysis and guiding management. Collectively, these entities illustrate the clinical diversity encountered in infancy, where infectious, hemorrhagic, and hemolytic processes intersect, and underscore the importance of precise diagnostic categorization and laboratory assessment for optimal newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 214
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a neonatal hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease spectrum that includes specific syndromes (Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome, Ingkikte‑galsyndroom) and conditions such as bacterial sepsis of the newborn (unspecified). This overarching condition, termed NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN, comprises multiple bleeding complications—including losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding (umbilical cord clamp loosening with bleeding), نزف سري بعد الولادة (post‑natal umbilical bleeding), and نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding)—and a variety of jaundice phenotypes: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed development of the conjugating system, يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk jaundice), general يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (physiologic jaundice associated with preterm birth). These entities illustrate how hemorrhagic, hemolytic, and conjugation‑defect mechanisms interrelate in newborns, emphasizing the need for integrated recognition, assessment of underlying cause and gestational age, and coordinated management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 985
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal period encompasses a broad spectrum of hemorrhagic, hemolytic and jaundice disorders. Within the neonatal qualifier, bacterial sepsis of the newborn, unspecified, is recorded, and Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome is classified under Neonatal Hemorrhage and Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn. Neonatal Hemorrhage and Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn includes entities such as neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, neonatal jaundice due to delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), jaundice caused by epidermal barrier deficiency in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and prematurity‑associated neonatal jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Hemorrhagic complications listed in the same category are loosened umbilical cord clamp with bleeding (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding), post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة), and neonatal respiratory‑tract bleeding (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). The presence of hemorrhage and hemolysis in the newborn increases bilirubin accumulation, particularly in preterm infants, thereby heightening the risk of prematurity‑associated neonatal jaundice; consequently, careful monitoring and timely treatment of jaundice are essential for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1095
retrieved_docs.content: Bacteriële sepsis van pasgeborene, niet gespecificeerd is identified as a neonatal condition, indicating that this form of bacterial sepsis occurs in the newborn period. The neonatal qualifier also encompasses a spectrum of other newborn complications. Losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding, described in Dutch as bleeding from a loose umbilical clamp, is a neonatal event, as is نزف سري بعد الولادة, the Arabic term for post‑delivery umbilical bleeding. Additionally, نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ, which refers to hemorrhage of the neonatal respiratory tract, falls under the same neonatal category. Two distinct forms of neonatal jaundice are also included: يرقان حليب الثدي, or breast‑milk jaundice, and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان, jaundice associated with preterm birth. Together, these entities illustrate the range of infectious, hemorrhagic, and bilirubin‑related disorders that can arise during the neonatal period, underscoring the importance of vigilant monitoring and targeted management of newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 1077
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to several related neonatal conditions and a bleeding complication. It encompasses neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, reflecting immature hepatic conjugation capacity. It also includes bleeding that can arise from a loose umbilical cord clamp (losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding). In addition, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي) may be observed, as well as physiologic jaundice of the newborn caused by immature conjugation in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد). Prematurity‑related jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان) is another manifestation associated with this condition. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal period, encompassing the fetal and post‑natal phases from birth to death, is a critical window for infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic disorders. Unspecified bacterial sepsis of the newborn and Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome are recorded in this interval, highlighting early‑life metabolic and enzymatic defects. Ingedikte‑galsyndroom and Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome are classified within the broader entity **Neonatal Hemorrhage and Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn**, which also comprises multiple jaundice phenotypes: neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation of bilirubin, delayed development of the conjugating system, breast‑milk jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), physiologic neonatal jaundice (يرقان نووي), jaundice caused by epidermal immaturity in the fetus or newborn (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and preterm‑associated jaundice (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Hemolytic jaundice and perinatal jaundice are key subcategories of this

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2522
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to several distinct clinical manifestations. It may present as losgeraakte navelstrengklem; bloeding (loose umbilical cord clamp with bleeding), as نزف سري بعد الولادة (umbilical bleeding after birth), as نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), and as two types of jaundice: يرقان حليب الثدي (breast‑milk‑induced jaundice) and يرقان نووي (physiologic newborn jaundice). Recognizing these related presentations is essential for comprehensive evaluation and appropriate management of affected newborns.
